title: Modulation Techniques
description: Methods of encoding digital data onto carrier waves for transmission over wireless or analog media.
fullExplanation: Modulation encodes digital data onto a carrier wave by varying its amplitude, frequency, or phase. More complex modulation schemes (higher QAM orders) pack more bits per symbol but require a cleaner signal. WiFi and cellular networks use OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing) which splits the channel into hundreds of narrow subcarriers, each independently modulated — this makes the signal robust against multipath interference.

techniques.name: ASK (Amplitude Shift Keying)
techniques.description: Vary amplitude to represent bits. Simple but noise-sensitive. Used in optical fiber (on/off).

techniques.name: FSK (Frequency Shift Keying)
techniques.description: Vary frequency. More noise-resistant than ASK. Used in old modems.

techniques.name: PSK (Phase Shift Keying)
techniques.description: Vary phase. BPSK=1 bit/symbol, QPSK=2 bits/symbol. Used in satellite, WiFi.

techniques.name: QAM (Quadrature Amplitude Modulation)
techniques.description: Vary both amplitude and phase. 16-QAM=4 bits, 64-QAM=6 bits, 1024-QAM=10 bits per symbol.

techniques.name: OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)
techniques.description: Splits channel into many narrow subcarriers. Each uses QAM. Robust against multipath. Used in WiFi, LTE, 5G, DSL.

techniques.name: OFDMA
techniques.description: OFDM + multiple access — different users get different subcarriers simultaneously. WiFi 6, LTE.

keyFacts: Higher QAM = more bits per symbol but needs better SNR
OFDM uses cyclic prefix to handle multipath reflections
WiFi adapts modulation dynamically based on signal quality (MCS index)
Shannon limit: theoretical maximum bits/s = B × log2(1 + SNR)
Guard intervals in OFDM prevent inter-symbol interference
MIMO + OFDM = massive throughput gains in modern WiFi and 5G
